content.problemStatement:
The alternating sum of an array AAA of length NNN is defined to be

∑i=1N(−1)i+1Ai\sum_{i=1}^N (-1)^{i+1} A_ii=1∑N​(−1)i+1Ai​If you expand it out, this is the expression A1−A2+A3−A4+…+(−1)N+1ANA_1 - A_2 + A_3 - A_4 + \ldots + (-1)^{N+1} A_NA1​−A2​+A3​−A4​+…+(−1)N+1AN​.

You're given an array AAA containing NNN integers. You can rearrange this array however you like.

Find the **maximum** possible value of the alternating sum of AAA after rearrangement.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the maximum possible alternating sum after rearranging AAA.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal rearrangement is [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3], which gives an alternating sum of 2−1+3=42 - 1 + 3 = 42−1+3=4.
Test case 222: No matter how the array is rearranged, its alternating sum is 5−5+5−5=05-5+5-5 = 05−5+5−5=0.
Test case 333: One optimal rearrangement is [4,3,9,1,5,1][4, 3, 9, 1, 5, 1][4,3,9,1,5,1], which gives an alternating sum of 4−3+9−1+5−1=134-3+9-1+5-1 = 134−3+9−1+5−1=13.
